mnemonics.title: NIST Cloud Characteristics
mnemonics.content: The five characteristics can be remembered using the acronym 'OBERM': On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Elasticity, Resource pooling, Measured service. Think 'Ocean Birds Easily Rise Mounting' - O(cean) B(irds) E(asily) R(ise) M(ounting).

mnemonics.title: Cloud Service Models
mnemonics.content: IaaS: Infrastructure = Hardware you rent. PaaS: Platform = Dev environment. SaaS: Software = Apps you use. Remember: 'I Pay Six Annually' - I (Infrastructure), P (Platform), S (Software). The more you move up, the less you manage, the more you pay.

mnemonics.title: Cloud Deployment Models
mnemonics.content: Public is for everyone, Private for one, Hybrid mixes both, Community shares concerns. Remember: 'Pure Hybrid Community' - P(ublic) H(ybrid) C(ommunity) - think of letters representing first letters.

mnemonics.title: Shared Responsibility Model
mnemonics.content: In IaaS: Provider does Physical-Hypervisor-Network. Customer does OS-Apps-Data. In SaaS: Provider does almost everything. Remember: 'P-H-N' for Provider (Physical, Hypervisor, Network) and 'O-A-D' for Customer (OS, Apps, Data).

acronyms.usage: Company providing cloud services like AWS, Azure, Google Cloud
Tools that continuously monitor cloud environments for misconfigurations and compliance issues
Security software that enforces policies between cloud users and service providers
Outsourced security services delivered over the internet by third-party providers
Organization promoting best practices for cloud security and offering STAR certification
Framework for managing digital identities and access rights in cloud environments
Security requiring two or more verification methods to access an account
Access management method assigning permissions based on organizational roles

visualTips.visualization: Picture a layered cake. The bottom layer (physical infrastructure) is provider's responsibility. Each layer above (hypervisor, OS, apps, data) is customer's responsibility as you move from IaaS to SaaS. In SaaS, provider handles almost all layers except top (user/data).
Think of a house: IaaS = empty lot where you build, PaaS = foundation + frame where you build the house, SaaS = fully furnished house you just move into. More managed = less control but easier setup.

Imagine an apartment building (cloud). Each tenant (customer) has their own locked apartment (data isolation), but they share the building infrastructure. Security ensures one tenant can't enter another's apartment.
Public = hotel (shared by anyone), Private = your own house, Community = shared apartment with friends, Hybrid = your house + hotel depending on needs.

analogies.analogy: Like electricity - you use what you need, pay for what you use, don't worry about power plant maintenance.
Like a apartment building converting one large floor into multiple smaller units - maximizing resource utilization while maintaining privacy.
Like renting an apartment: landlord (provider) maintains building structure, plumbing, electricity; tenant (customer) maintains their unit, locks, belongings.
Like a bank: physical vault (provider security), but you still need your PIN, ID, and can't share account details (customer security). Both layers needed.
